title: Walnut fudge
subtitle: Classic British soft sugar candy with walnuts beaten to a smooth, dense texture
excerpt: Traditional British fudge made from sugar, butter, and double cream cooked to the soft-ball stage and beaten off the heat until it thickens to a dense, smooth, melt-in-the-mouth consistency. Chopped walnuts add crunch and a slightly bitter note that balances the sweetness. Softer and creamier than Scottish tablet, with a smooth rather than grainy texture. Cut into small squares; it is very rich.
sourceNotes: Fudge is a British and American confection documented in cookery literature from the late nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (various editions, Project Gutenberg) includes cream-and-sugar candy preparations consistent with fudge. The technique of cooking sugar and cream to the soft-ball stage and beating the mixture off the heat to create a smooth candy texture is described in British confectionery literature of the late Victorian period. Walnut fudge became a particularly popular Christmas confection in the twentieth century.

content.text: is the target; a sugar thermometer is the reliable way to hit it. Beat the mixture until it just starts to thicken and lose its gloss, then add the walnuts and pour. Over-beating produces a dry, crumbly fudge that does not set smooth.

Line the tin with baking paper. Combine the sugar, cream, and butter in a medium saucepan. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves. Bring to the boil, clip on the sugar thermometer, and cook over medium heat, stirring frequently, until the mixture reaches 116°C. This takes about 15 to 20 minutes. Do not let it boil dry or scorch on the base.

Remove from the heat and add the vanilla and salt. Leave to cool without stirring until the temperature drops to about 60°C (feel the outside of the pan; it should be warm but not burning). Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon for 5 to 8 minutes until the mixture thickens, becomes less glossy, and looks creamy. Stir in the chopped walnuts. Pour into the tin and spread level. Leave to set at room temperature for at least 2 hours before cutting into squares.
